result.text: What Texas Tech basketball's Christian Anderson, LeJuan Watts said of injuries Skip to main content Home PGA Tour LPGA College Amateur Golf Life Equipment Apparel Newsletter Videos Events Odds Betting USA TODAY Sports Contact Us Texas Tech basketball players offer injury updates after Big 12 Tournament exits Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal KANSAS CITY, Mo. — In the span of about 30 seconds in Thursday's quarterfinal game of the Big 12 Tournament, the Texas Tech basketball team saw Christian Anderson and LeJuan Watts both leave the T-Mobile Center floor with apparent injuries. Watts' came first after he went down in pain holding his foot after an awkward landing. On the next possession, Anderson slipped on the court for the umpteenth time, stayed on the ground and was taken to the end of the bench. By the time of both injuries, Iowa State had opened up the game and coasted to a 75-53 win to advance to the semifinals to send Texas Tech packing. Watts and Anderson both took their regular places on the bench after getting looked at but neither returned to the game. "I'm feeling good," Anderson said in the postgame press conference. "Obviously, the floor is a bit slippery so I think I just kind of mis-stepped or did a movement that caused me to kind of slip and ended up in a little unnatural position. That's what it was." Watts, meanwhile, isn't even entirely sure how he suffered his injury. "I actually don't know," Watts said in the locker room. "We're gonna have to go back. It was either step on someone's ankle or the court, maybe. I'm not sure." Watts also said, "I think I'm just gonna do some rehab for a few days and bounce back." Texas Tech heads to the NCAA Tournament next week with their tournament seed, destination and opponent to be announced Sunday. result.text: Akron will take third straight trip to March Madness as MAC champion Skip to main content Home PGA Tour LPGA College Amateur Golf Life Equipment Apparel Newsletter Videos Events Odds Betting USA TODAY Sports Contact Us Shammah Scott's 3 lifts Akron to MAC Men's Basketball Tournament title Michael Beaven Akron Beacon Journal CLEVELAND — The University of Akron men's basketball team found another way to win a Mid-American Conference Tournament championship in dramatic fashion for the third year in a row Saturday, March 14 at Rocket Arena. Akron graduate student Shammah Scott joined former Zips standouts Greg Tribble Jr. and Nate Johnson in making a last-second game-winning shot to clinch a MAC Tournament title and a spot in the NCAA Tournament. Scott swished a 3-pointer from about NBA range with 6.0 seconds remaining to lift No. 2 seed Akron to a 79-76 win over No. 4 seed Toledo before a crowd of 11,072. Scott's 3 to beat Toledo in the 2026 MAC Tournament final came after Johnson made a floating layup with 2.3 seconds remaining to give Akron a 76-74 win over Miami in the 2025 final and Tribble made two free throws with 4.8 seconds left to lift Akron to a 62-61 win over Kent State in the 2024 final. “I knew I had to make a play,” said Scott, a 6-foot-2 guard and a two-time MAC Sixth Man of the Year . “Going into the huddle, the coaches put a lot of trust in me, so I just wanted to come through for my coaches and my teammates. I love all these dudes and I wouldn't want to win with nobody else. “… It's a very cool moment. This is definitely something that I dreamed of, and I'm just blessed and grateful to be put in this situation. Words can’t explain what I'm feeling right now.” Toledo (19-15) almost forced overtime when Rockets junior center Austin Parks attempted an off-balance desperation 3-pointer that hit off the back of the rim as time expired. MAC Freshman of the Year Leroy Blyden Jr. dribbled the ball up the court after Scott's 3, but lost control of it when Akron senior Evan Mahaffey poked it toward's Toledo's bench, where Parks was standing. "I'm tired," Akron coach John Groce said. "Unbelievable night. ... It's such a privilege to coach those guys. “We wanted to get the ball in his [Scott's] hands there and make sure that we, as much as we could there was a little bit of a differential in game clock and shot clock, but we wanted to give ourselves the best chance to have the last shot. With the ball in his hands, obviously we trust him, his teammates trust him, and he made a big shot.” Akron (29-5) is the first team to earn a three-peat as the champion of the MAC Men's Basketball Tournament since the event started in 1980. Toledo won the first MAC Tournament title and is still searching for its second crown. “It's unfathomable,” Groce said of the three-peat. “You think about all the great coaches and great players that have been in this league, the ones that are currently in the league, and how long the league's been around. It's humbling.”

result.text: Texas Tech basketball feeling impact of JT Toppin's absence more and more Skip to main content Home Schedule NFL News Draft Fantasy Odds Betting Newsletter USA TODAY Sports How Texas Tech basketball's loss to BYU should cement JT Toppin as Big 12 POY Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal Despite the team's 3-0 record immediately after losing JT Toppin to a season-ending ACL tear , the Texas Tech basketball team was never better without the reigning Big 12 player of the year on the floor. Timing is the biggest issue of Toppin's absence. Being so close to March Madness, with the NCAA Tournament on the horizon, the Red Raiders have had to adjust everything from how they start their offense to how they cover ball screens on defense, and had five regular-season games to do it before the postseason arrived. The first three games were a breath of fresh air, showing what other players on the team are capable of while giving opponents a new look from their typical scouting report of "find Toppin, try to limit him." Kansas State and Cincinnati didn't have the luxury of advance scouting, and Iowa State probably felt confident it would get the job done at home. The Cyclones were wrong. TCU and BYU, meanwhile, had a three-game run-up to see what this version of the Red Raiders looks like. And the secret is out: They're not physically tough and can be pushed around. Christian Anderson has a tendency to shy away the paint, even if Texas Tech needs something going to the basket or even if the layup is wide open to take. He started turning it over much more without Toppin as the safety valve. Luke Bamgboye and Josiah Moseley have not been anywhere as lethal with the ball in their hands. BYU decided not to trap Anderson, instead switching every ball screen — real or bluffed — and putting the 6-foot-3 point guard into positions to be a 1-on-1 scorer. Anderson made them pay in the first half but went quiet until the final few minutes as Texas Tech was forging a comeback attempt. result.text: Akron will take third straight trip to March Madness as MAC champion Skip to main content Home PGA Tour LPGA College Amateur Golf Life Equipment Apparel Newsletter Videos Events Odds Betting USA TODAY Sports Contact Us Shammah Scott's 3 lifts Akron to MAC Men's Basketball Tournament title Michael Beaven Akron Beacon Journal CLEVELAND — The University of Akron men's basketball team found another way to win a Mid-American Conference Tournament championship in dramatic fashion for the third year in a row Saturday, March 14 at Rocket Arena. Akron graduate student Shammah Scott joined former Zips standouts Greg Tribble Jr. and Nate Johnson in making a last-second game-winning shot to clinch a MAC Tournament title and a spot in the NCAA Tournament. Scott swished a 3-pointer from about NBA range with 6.0 seconds remaining to lift No. 2 seed Akron to a 79-76 win over No. 4 seed Toledo before a crowd of 11,072. Scott's 3 to beat Toledo in the 2026 MAC Tournament final came after Johnson made a floating layup with 2.3 seconds remaining to give Akron a 76-74 win over Miami in the 2025 final and Tribble made two free throws with 4.8 seconds left to lift Akron to a 62-61 win over Kent State in the 2024 final. “I knew I had to make a play,” said Scott, a 6-foot-2 guard and a two-time MAC Sixth Man of the Year . “Going into the huddle, the coaches put a lot of trust in me, so I just wanted to come through for my coaches and my teammates. I love all these dudes and I wouldn't want to win with nobody else. “… It's a very cool moment. This is definitely something that I dreamed of, and I'm just blessed and grateful to be put in this situation. Words can’t explain what I'm feeling right now.” Toledo (19-15) almost forced overtime when Rockets junior center Austin Parks attempted an off-balance desperation 3-pointer that hit off the back of the rim as time expired. MAC Freshman of the Year Leroy Blyden Jr. dribbled the ball up the court after Scott's 3, but lost control of it when Akron senior Evan Mahaffey poked it toward's Toledo's bench, where Parks was standing. "I'm tired," Akron coach John Groce said. "Unbelievable night. ... It's such a privilege to coach those guys. “We wanted to get the ball in his [Scott's] hands there and make sure that we, as much as we could there was a little bit of a differential in game clock and shot clock, but we wanted to give ourselves the best chance to have the last shot. With the ball in his hands, obviously we trust him, his teammates trust him, and he made a big shot.” Akron (29-5) is the first team to earn a three-peat as the champion of the MAC Men's Basketball Tournament since the event started in 1980. Toledo won the first MAC Tournament title and is still searching for its second crown. “It's unfathomable,” Groce said of the three-peat. “You think about all the great coaches and great players that have been in this league, the ones that are currently in the league, and how long the league's been around. It's humbling.”

result.text: Texas Tech basketball feeling impact of JT Toppin's absence more and more Skip to main content Home Schedule NFL News Draft Fantasy Odds Betting Newsletter USA TODAY Sports How Texas Tech basketball's loss to BYU should cement JT Toppin as Big 12 POY Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal Despite the team's 3-0 record immediately after losing JT Toppin to a season-ending ACL tear , the Texas Tech basketball team was never better without the reigning Big 12 player of the year on the floor. Timing is the biggest issue of Toppin's absence. Being so close to March Madness, with the NCAA Tournament on the horizon, the Red Raiders have had to adjust everything from how they start their offense to how they cover ball screens on defense, and had five regular-season games to do it before the postseason arrived. The first three games were a breath of fresh air, showing what other players on the team are capable of while giving opponents a new look from their typical scouting report of "find Toppin, try to limit him." Kansas State and Cincinnati didn't have the luxury of advance scouting, and Iowa State probably felt confident it would get the job done at home. The Cyclones were wrong. TCU and BYU, meanwhile, had a three-game run-up to see what this version of the Red Raiders looks like. And the secret is out: They're not physically tough and can be pushed around. Christian Anderson has a tendency to shy away the paint, even if Texas Tech needs something going to the basket or even if the layup is wide open to take. He started turning it over much more without Toppin as the safety valve. Luke Bamgboye and Josiah Moseley have not been anywhere as lethal with the ball in their hands. BYU decided not to trap Anderson, instead switching every ball screen — real or bluffed — and putting the 6-foot-3 point guard into positions to be a 1-on-1 scorer. Anderson made them pay in the first half but went quiet until the final few minutes as Texas Tech was forging a comeback attempt. Nobody's going to point the finger at Anderson (23 points, 9 assists) for the 82-76 loss to the Cougars on Saturday, March 7, nor should they. That loss, like the TCU setback, can be attributed to one glaring issue. The frontcourt has gone soft. It wasn't the most physical team in the country even with Toppin, and now that he's gone there's little resistance in the paint. Texas Tech was outscored 48-14 in the paint, giving up 20 offense rebounds to BYU, which turned them into another 20 points. Same song and dance as the TCU game. Push came to shove, and the Red Raiders were put into the mat. While there were many things that came out of Toppin's lost final month of the season, one of the big arguments was what happens to the Big 12 player of the year award? Toppin was the clear front-runner to repeat as the recipient, so with him out the equation, who would it go to? AJ Dybantsa, who leads the country in scoring (and complaining about fouls)?
